patterns.name: Factory Method
patterns.intent: Define an interface for creating an object, but let subclasses decide which class to instantiate.
patterns.description: The Factory Method pattern defines a method (the 'factory method') in a base class or interface whose purpose is to create objects. Subclasses override this method to change the type of objects that will be created. The base class calls the factory method to get instances rather than constructing them directly with 'new'. This decouples the creation logic from the business logic and makes the system open for extension without modification of existing code.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when a class cannot anticipate the class of objects it must create, when subclasses should control which objects get created, or when you want to localise knowledge of which class gets created. Common in frameworks where the framework provides the skeleton but leaves concrete class selection to the application code. Avoid if there is only ever one concrete type — that is unnecessary complexity.

patterns.name: Abstract Factory
patterns.intent: Provide an interface for creating families of related or dependent objects without specifying their concrete classes.
patterns.description: Abstract Factory groups a set of factory methods behind a single interface, each creating a different product in a family. The client code works against the abstract interface and never references concrete product classes directly. Swapping out one factory implementation replaces the entire product family atomically — useful when a system must be configured to work with one of multiple product families (e.g. UI toolkits, database drivers, cloud providers).
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when the system needs to be independent of how its products are created, composed, and represented, and when you need to enforce constraints that products in a family are used together. Also useful for test doubles — provide a TestFactory that returns mocks instead of real objects. Avoid when there is only one product family; a simple Factory Method is sufficient.

patterns.name: Builder
patterns.intent: Separate the construction of a complex object from its representation.
patterns.description: Builder breaks the construction of a complex object into a series of method calls on a Builder object, culminating in a terminal 'build()' call that returns the assembled product. This avoids telescoping constructors (constructors with many optional parameters), keeps objects immutable after construction, and makes it easy to produce different representations from the same construction sequence. Commonly seen as a fluent API (method chaining) in modern codebases.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when constructing complex objects that require many optional configuration parameters, when the construction process should allow different representations of the constructed object, or when you want to enforce that an object is only created in a valid state. Particularly useful for value objects, query builders, HTTP request builders, and test fixture builders. Avoid for simple objects with two or fewer fields.

patterns.name: Singleton
patterns.intent: Ensure a class has only one instance and provide a global point of access to it.
patterns.description: Singleton restricts instantiation of a class to a single object, typically by making the constructor private and exposing a static getInstance() method. In modern Java applications this is usually delegated to a DI container (Spring @Component, @Singleton annotation) rather than implemented manually. The manual double-checked locking pattern is notoriously error-prone prior to Java 5.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use for stateless service objects, shared caches, thread pools, or registries where a single shared instance is both necessary and sufficient. In Spring/CDI applications prefer container-managed singletons over manual implementation. Avoid for objects that hold mutable state shared across threads — this becomes a concurrency bottleneck and makes testing harder because tests cannot easily reset shared state.

patterns.name: Prototype
patterns.intent: Specify the kinds of objects to create using a prototypical instance and create new objects by copying this prototype.
patterns.description: Prototype creates new objects by cloning an existing object (the prototype) rather than calling a constructor. In Java this typically means implementing java.lang.Cloneable and overriding clone(). The pattern avoids expensive re-initialisation costs and allows runtime configuration of product types without subclassing. Deep vs shallow clone semantics must be carefully managed.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when object creation is expensive (e.g. involves database queries, network calls, or complex setup) and you need many similar objects. Also useful when the classes to instantiate are specified at runtime, or when you want to avoid building a class hierarchy of factories. In modern Java, consider copy constructors or static factory methods as safer alternatives to Cloneable.

patterns.name: Adapter
patterns.intent: Convert the interface of a class into another interface clients expect.
patterns.description: Adapter (also called Wrapper) lets classes with incompatible interfaces work together. The adapter class implements the target interface and holds a reference to an adaptee object, delegating calls and translating between the two interfaces. There are two forms: class adapter (uses multiple inheritance, possible in C++) and object adapter (uses composition, the standard Java approach). Adapters are pervasive at integration boundaries — wrapping third-party libraries, legacy systems, or external APIs.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when you want to use an existing class but its interface does not match what you need, when you need to create a reusable class that cooperates with unrelated classes that have incompatible interfaces, or when adapting several existing subclasses would require a lot of subclassing. Common at anti-corruption layers and when integrating external SDKs. Avoid if you can change the original class — an adapter adds indirection.

patterns.name: Composite
patterns.intent: Compose objects into tree structures to represent part-whole hierarchies.
patterns.description: Composite lets clients treat individual objects (leaves) and compositions of objects (composites) uniformly through a shared component interface. The composite class holds a collection of child components and implements the component interface by delegating to its children. This is the backbone of UI widget trees, file systems, expression trees, and organisation charts.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when you need to represent part-whole hierarchies of objects and want clients to be able to ignore the difference between compositions of objects and individual objects. Use when the core model can be represented as a tree. Avoid when the hierarchy is shallow or when leaf and composite objects have fundamentally different operations that cannot be unified — forcing a common interface can lead to methods that make no sense on one type.

patterns.name: Decorator
patterns.intent: Attach additional responsibilities to an object dynamically (class-based wrapper pattern).
patterns.description: Decorator wraps an object with another object that implements the same interface, adding behaviour before or after delegating to the wrapped object. Decorators can be stacked — each layer adds one responsibility — making it easy to compose cross-cutting concerns like logging, caching, authentication, and compression without modifying the original class. In Java this is the foundation of java.io (BufferedInputStream wrapping FileInputStream, etc.).
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when you need to add responsibilities to individual objects dynamically and transparently without affecting other objects, or when extension by subclassing is impractical (e.g. would require an explosion of subclasses). Prefer over subclassing when behaviours can be composed orthogonally. Note: in Python the @decorator syntax is a different concept (function wrapping, not this pattern). Avoid when the wrapped interface is very large — maintaining delegation boilerplate becomes burdensome.

patterns.name: Facade
patterns.intent: Provide a simplified interface to a complex subsystem.
patterns.description: Facade defines a higher-level interface that makes a subsystem easier to use by hiding its complexity behind a single entry point. The facade delegates client requests to the appropriate subsystem objects and manages their lifecycles and interdependencies. It does not add new behaviour — it simplifies access. Facades are common in service layers (a single OrderService that orchestrates inventory, payment, and shipping subsystems).
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when you want to provide a simple interface to a complex subsystem, when there are many dependencies between clients and implementation classes, or when you want to layer your subsystems. Facades are excellent for defining entry points to each subsystem layer. Avoid mistaking facade for a dumping ground — if the facade grows to have business logic of its own, it becomes a god class.

patterns.name: Proxy
patterns.intent: Provide a surrogate or placeholder for another object to control access to it.
patterns.description: Proxy stands in for another object and controls access to it. Common proxy types: Remote proxy (represents an object in a different address space), Virtual proxy (lazy-initialises an expensive object on first use), Protection proxy (checks permissions before delegating), Caching proxy (stores results and returns cached copies). In Java, dynamic proxies (java.lang.reflect.Proxy) and AOP frameworks (Spring AOP) generate proxies at runtime.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use for lazy initialisation of expensive resources, adding access control without modifying the real subject, caching remote or expensive results, and logging/monitoring without modifying business logic. AOP-based proxies are the standard approach in Spring for @Transactional, @Cacheable, and security annotations. Avoid manual proxy classes when a framework can generate them dynamically.

patterns.name: Chain of Responsibility
patterns.intent: Avoid coupling the sender of a request to its receiver by giving more than one object a chance to handle the request.
patterns.description: Chain of Responsibility passes a request along a chain of handlers. Each handler decides either to process the request or to pass it to the next handler in the chain. This decouples senders from receivers and allows the chain to be assembled dynamically at runtime. It is the structural backbone of servlet filters, Django middleware, Express.js middleware, and event bubbling in UI frameworks.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when more than one object may handle a request, and the handler is not known a priori — it should be determined at runtime. Use when you want to issue a request to one of several objects without specifying the receiver explicitly, or when the set of objects that can handle a request should be specified dynamically. Common for authentication, validation, logging, and rate-limiting pipelines. Avoid when the chain is always the same fixed set — a simple conditional or strategy is clearer.

patterns.name: Command
patterns.intent: Encapsulate a request as an object, thereby letting you parameterise clients with different requests.
patterns.description: Command encapsulates all information needed to perform an action or trigger an event into a standalone object. This includes the method to call, the object that owns the method, and the parameters. Command objects can be queued, logged, undone, and retried. They are the foundation of undo/redo systems, job queues, transactional outboxes, and CQRS command buses.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when you need to parameterise objects with operations, queue operations for deferred execution, support undo/redo, implement transactional behaviour, or build audit logs of operations. Command pattern is central to CQRS architectures. Also useful for multi-step wizards where each step is a reversible command. Avoid for simple one-off method calls where the overhead of an object adds no value.

patterns.name: Iterator
patterns.intent: Provide a way to access elements of a collection sequentially without exposing its underlying representation.
patterns.description: Iterator provides a standard way to traverse a collection without exposing its internal structure (array, tree, hash map, etc.). In Java, java.util.Iterator is built into the language and all collections implement Iterable. Custom iterators are needed for non-standard data structures such as trees, graphs, lazy sequences, and database result streams. The enhanced for-loop (for-each) relies on Iterable under the hood.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when you need to traverse a collection without knowing its internal representation, when you want to support multiple simultaneous traversals (each iterator holds its own cursor), or when you are implementing a custom data structure that should integrate with the Java for-each loop. In modern Java, prefer Streams API for functional-style traversal. Avoid implementing Iterator manually for standard collections — Java's built-in implementations are sufficient.

patterns.name: Observer
patterns.intent: Define a one-to-many dependency between objects so that when one object changes state, all its dependants are notified.
patterns.description: Observer (also known as Publish-Subscribe or Event Listener) defines a subject that maintains a list of observers and notifies them automatically when its state changes. Observers register themselves with the subject and are decoupled from each other — the subject does not need to know what the observers do with the notification. This is the core mechanism behind GUI event handling, reactive streams, domain events, and message brokers.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when a change in one object requires changing others and you do not know how many objects need to change, or when an object should be able to notify other objects without making assumptions about who those objects are. Common in event-driven architectures, UI frameworks, and domain-driven design (domain events). Consider reactive libraries (RxJava, Reactor) for complex event processing. Avoid if observers need guaranteed ordering — basic observer does not guarantee delivery order.

patterns.name: Strategy
patterns.intent: Define a family of algorithms, encapsulate each one, and make them interchangeable.
patterns.description: Strategy defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each one in a class that implements a common interface, and makes them interchangeable at runtime. The context object holds a reference to a strategy and delegates the algorithm to it. This eliminates conditional statements that select between algorithms and makes it easy to add new algorithms without modifying existing code. In Python and Java 8+, strategies can be represented as lambdas or functional interfaces, avoiding separate classes.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when you need to switch between different variants of an algorithm at runtime, when you want to eliminate conditional logic that selects behaviour, or when different classes differ only in their behaviour. Classic examples: sorting algorithms, payment methods, compression formats, pricing rules. In languages with first-class functions (Python, modern Java), a plain callable is often simpler than a full Strategy class — reserve the class-based form for strategies with state or multiple methods.

patterns.name: Template Method
patterns.intent: Define the skeleton of an algorithm in an operation, deferring some steps to subclasses.
patterns.description: Template Method defines the overall structure (skeleton) of an algorithm in a base class method, leaving specific steps to be filled in by subclasses. The base class calls abstract or overridable 'hook' methods at the appropriate points. Subclasses override the hooks but cannot change the algorithm's overall structure. This is one of the most commonly used patterns in Java frameworks — Spring's JdbcTemplate, TestCase base classes, and Servlet lifecycle all use it.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when you have invariant parts of an algorithm that should not change, but the details of some steps should vary across subclasses. Good for frameworks that define the skeleton and leave application-specific steps to the user. Also useful for enforcing pre/post conditions around overridable steps. Prefer composition (Strategy) over Template Method when the variation points are numerous or need to be swapped at runtime, since inheritance creates tight coupling.

patterns.name: Visitor
patterns.intent: Represent an operation to be performed on elements of an object structure without changing the classes of the elements.
patterns.description: Visitor lets you add new operations to an object structure without modifying the classes of the elements. Elements implement an accept(visitor) method; the visitor implements a visit method for each concrete element type. Double dispatch ensures the correct visitor method is called based on both the visitor type and the element type. Compilers, linters, and static analysis tools make heavy use of Visitor to traverse ASTs.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when you need to perform many distinct and unrelated operations on an object structure without polluting their classes, and when the object structure classes are stable but new operations are added frequently. Classic domains: AST processing, document rendering, serialisation. Avoid when the element class hierarchy changes often — every new element class requires updating all visitor implementations. Also avoid if the element types are not known in advance.

patterns.name: State
patterns.intent: Allow an object to alter its behaviour when its internal state changes.
patterns.description: State encapsulates state-dependent behaviour in separate State classes. The context object holds a reference to the current state and delegates behaviour to it. Transitions happen by replacing the state object. This eliminates large if/switch statements that branch on an internal status field and makes it easy to add new states without modifying existing code. Finite state machines (order workflows, connection lifecycles, game AI) are natural fits.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when an object's behaviour depends on its state and must change at runtime, especially when there are many states and state-specific behaviour. If you have if/switch blocks that branch on an enum field throughout multiple methods of a class, State is likely the right refactoring. Avoid for objects with only two or three simple states — a boolean or enum with a few conditionals is simpler. In Python, state machines are often implemented with dictionaries or libraries (transitions) rather than class hierarchies.

patterns.name: Message Handler
patterns.intent: Process messages arriving on a channel and dispatch them to the appropriate component.
patterns.description: Message Handler (also called Message Endpoint in EIP) is the code that connects an application to the messaging system. It reads messages off a channel, transforms them into domain objects, and delegates to application logic. It is responsible for acknowledgement, error handling, and dead-letter routing. In Java this maps to @MessageListener, @KafkaListener, @SqsListener, and similar annotations.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use whenever an application needs to consume messages from a broker (Kafka, RabbitMQ, SQS, JMS). Handlers should be thin — they unmarshal the message and call a domain service; they should not contain business logic themselves. Apply idempotency guards in the handler when the messaging system delivers at-least-once. Separate the handler class from the business service so the business logic remains testable without a broker.

patterns.name: Aggregator
patterns.intent: Collect and store individual but related messages until a complete set has been received, then publish a single distilled message.
patterns.description: The Aggregator collects messages that belong together (based on a correlation ID or grouping key) and waits until a completeness condition is satisfied before emitting a single combined message downstream. It needs persistent state (a message store) to hold partial aggregates across restarts. Common completeness conditions: all N messages received, a timeout elapsed, or a special 'end' message arrived.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when you need to combine multiple related messages from different sources or time windows into one — e.g. collecting all line items before processing an order, batching sensor readings, or waiting for all microservice responses before replying to a client (scatter-gather). Requires careful thought about failure and timeout handling: what happens if one message never arrives? Avoid if messages are always received atomically — a simple join is sufficient.

patterns.name: Pipeline
patterns.intent: Process a message by passing it through a sequence of processing steps, each performing a specific function.
patterns.description: Pipeline (Pipes and Filters in EIP) structures a processing flow as a sequence of discrete filter components connected by channels (pipes). Each filter is independent, has a single responsibility, and communicates only via its input/output channels. The pipeline is assembled by connecting filters, making it easy to reorder, replace, or parallelize steps. Apache Camel, Spring Integration, and Kafka Streams are built on this pattern.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when a task can be decomposed into independent sequential steps with well-defined data flowing between them — ETL pipelines, document processing, image transformation, CI/CD stages. Each step should have a single responsibility and be independently testable. Avoid when steps have complex interdependencies or when the overhead of serialising and deserialising between steps outweighs the benefits of separation.

patterns.name: Content-Based Router
patterns.intent: Route each message to the correct recipient based on message content.
patterns.description: Content-Based Router examines the content (or headers) of an incoming message and routes it to one of several output channels. It is the messaging equivalent of a switch/if-else statement but operates at the infrastructure level, keeping routing logic out of business services. Routers can be chained, and routing tables can be updated at runtime without redeploying services.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when messages of different types arrive on a single channel and need to be dispatched to different handlers. Common in event-driven architectures where a single event bus carries many event types, in API gateways that route to different microservices, and in ETL pipelines that branch on record type. Keep routing logic simple (based on a header or type field) — if the router needs to call business logic to decide, extract that logic into a separate enricher step first.

patterns.name: Message Transformer
patterns.intent: Transform the content of a message to match the data format required by the receiver.
patterns.description: Message Transformer modifies a message as it passes through the pipeline — translating data formats (JSON to XML), enriching messages with additional data, filtering out unnecessary fields, or normalising inconsistent input into a canonical form. Transformers are the 'T' in ETL and the backbone of anti-corruption layers between bounded contexts.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use at the boundaries between systems that use different data formats or vocabularies, and when incoming data must be normalised or enriched before further processing. Keep transformers pure (no side effects, no I/O) so they are easily testable. Separate enrichment (calling external services) from simple structural transformation — enrichers are harder to test and have different failure modes.

patterns.name: Context Manager
patterns.intent: Classes implementing __enter__ and __exit__ for use with the 'with' statement — safe resource acquisition and release.
patterns.description: Python's context manager protocol consists of __enter__ (acquires the resource and optionally returns it) and __exit__ (releases the resource, even if an exception occurred). The 'with' statement guarantees __exit__ is always called, making it the Pythonic way to handle any resource that needs deterministic cleanup: file handles, database connections, locks, temporary directories, mock patches, and transactions. Alternatively, the @contextmanager decorator from contextlib can create context managers from generator functions.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply whenever a class manages a resource that must be released or cleaned up — database sessions, network connections, file handles, locks, test mocks, timers, and temporary state changes. Prefer contextlib.contextmanager for simple cases to avoid boilerplate. For classes that already have open/close semantics, adding __enter__/__exit__ is almost always the right call. Avoid using context managers to control non-resource state that should instead be handled by proper object lifecycle.

patterns.name: Descriptor
patterns.intent: Classes implementing __get__, __set__, or __delete__ to customise attribute access — the mechanism behind properties, classmethods, and ORM fields.
patterns.description: Descriptors are the low-level mechanism that powers Python's property(), classmethod(), staticmethod(), and ORM field declarations (Django's Field, SQLAlchemy's Column). A descriptor is a class that defines __get__, __set__, and/or __delete__. When an instance of such a class is assigned to a class attribute, attribute access on the owner class or its instances is intercepted. Data descriptors (with __set__) have higher lookup priority than instance __dict__; non-data descriptors (only __get__) have lower priority.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when you need reusable attribute-level validation, type coercion, lazy computation, or access control that should apply consistently across many classes. Descriptors are the right tool for building ORM field types, form field types, configuration attributes, and cached properties. For simple cases on a single class, @property is sufficient. Reach for a full descriptor class when the same logic needs to be reused across multiple classes.

patterns.name: Mixin
patterns.intent: Classes designed to be mixed into other classes to provide reusable behaviour without being a primary base class.
patterns.description: Mixins are classes that provide methods intended to be inherited by other classes but are not meant to be instantiated on their own. They rely on Python's multiple inheritance (MRO) to inject behaviour. A mixin should not inherit from the class it is designed to extend, should have a single responsibility, and typically does not define __init__. They are the Python alternative to Java interfaces with default methods or trait-based composition in Rust/Scala.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use to share behaviour across classes that do not share a common base class — e.g. adding JSON serialisation, timestamp fields, soft-delete logic, or permission checks to Django models. Name mixins with the 'Mixin' suffix to signal intent. Keep each mixin focused on one concern. Avoid creating deep mixin inheritance chains — the MRO becomes hard to reason about. For very simple shared behaviour, a standalone function or utility module may be clearer.

patterns.name: Abstract Base Class
patterns.intent: Classes inheriting from ABC or using ABCMeta to enforce interface contracts on subclasses.
patterns.description: Python's abc module provides ABCMeta and the ABC convenience base class. Decorating methods with @abstractmethod forces concrete subclasses to implement them, providing the same compile-time-like interface guarantees that Java interfaces offer. Abstract properties, class methods, and static methods are also supported. The abc.register() mechanism allows virtual subclassing — declaring that a class satisfies an interface without inheritance.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when defining a stable interface contract that multiple concrete implementations must satisfy — plugin interfaces, repository contracts, renderer protocols. ABCs make it clear which methods are mandatory and which are provided defaults. In modern Python (3.8+), consider typing.Protocol as an alternative for structural subtyping (duck typing with static checking), which avoids requiring explicit inheritance. Use ABC when you also want to provide default method implementations that subclasses can call via super().

patterns.name: Django Model
patterns.intent: Classes inheriting from django.db.models.Model — represent database tables and encapsulate query logic.
patterns.description: Django Models are the single definitive source of truth about your data. Each model class maps to a database table; each attribute maps to a column. Django auto-generates the schema via migrations. Models also carry validation logic (clean()), manager methods for custom queries, and Meta options for ordering, constraints, and permissions. They are the 'M' in Django's MTV (Model-Template-View) architecture.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply to any data entity that needs to be persisted to the database. Keep models focused on data and lightweight query helpers — avoid putting complex business logic directly in models (fat model anti-pattern). For read-heavy queries consider adding a custom Manager or QuerySet. For write logic, use service functions or domain objects that call model.save(). Always write migrations when changing model fields — never edit the database directly.

patterns.name: Django View / ViewSet
patterns.intent: Class-based views and DRF ViewSets that handle HTTP requests and return responses.
patterns.description: Django's class-based views (CBV) and Django REST Framework's ViewSets handle HTTP request routing and response generation. CBVs provide mixins for common operations (list, detail, create, update, delete). ViewSets group related views under a single class and integrate with DRF's router for automatic URL generation. They sit at the boundary between HTTP and the application's service/domain layer.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use class-based views when you need the mixins and inheritance provided by Django's CBV hierarchy, or when building a REST API with DRF. Keep views thin — they should authenticate, validate input (via serializers/forms), call a service function, and return a response. Do not put business logic in views. Use function-based views for simple one-off endpoints where CBV overhead adds no value.

patterns.name: Django Form / Serializer
patterns.intent: Form and Serializer classes handling input validation, cleaning, and rendering.
patterns.description: Django Forms validate and clean user-submitted data; DRF Serializers do the same for API input and also handle serialising Python objects to JSON. Both provide declarative field definitions, cross-field validation (clean()/validate()), and rich error messaging. ModelForms and ModelSerializers auto-derive fields from a Model, reducing boilerplate. They act as the application's input boundary, preventing invalid data from reaching the domain layer.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use for all user-supplied input — HTML forms, API request bodies, and query parameter parsing. Never access request.POST or request.data directly in a view without passing it through a Form or Serializer first. Write explicit validate_<field> and clean methods for business-rule validation. Prefer ModelSerializer for CRUD APIs but write custom Serializer classes for complex transformations or when the API shape diverges significantly from the model structure.

patterns.name: Django Middleware
patterns.intent: Middleware classes that wrap the request/response cycle to add cross-cutting concerns like authentication or caching.
patterns.description: Django middleware is a lightweight plugin system for globally altering Django's input or output. Each middleware component is a class with __init__(get_response) and __call__(request) methods. Middleware runs in order for requests (top-to-bottom in MIDDLEWARE setting) and in reverse for responses (bottom-to-top). Common uses: session handling, authentication, CSRF protection, request logging, rate limiting, content-language negotiation, and security headers.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use for concerns that must apply to every request (or a large subset) globally — authentication checks, security headers, request ID injection, performance tracing. Do not use middleware for logic that only applies to specific views — use decorators or mixins instead. Keep middleware lean: it runs on every request, so any overhead is multiplied. Avoid accessing the database in middleware unless absolutely necessary.

patterns.name: Django Manager
patterns.intent: Custom Manager classes that extend the default ORM query interface with domain-specific querysets.
patterns.description: Django Manager is the interface through which ORM queries are made. Every model has at least one Manager (objects by default). Custom Managers let you add domain-specific query methods (e.g. User.objects.active(), Order.objects.for_customer(customer)) and apply default filters (e.g. always filtering out soft-deleted records). Custom QuerySets are often paired with Managers to make query chaining work correctly.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use a custom Manager or QuerySet whenever you find yourself repeating the same filter() or exclude() logic in multiple views or services. Encapsulate the query logic once in the Manager and use it everywhere. Use QuerySet subclasses when you need chainable query methods. Add a custom Manager when you need to change the initial queryset (e.g. filtering by tenant). Always keep at least one Manager that returns all objects (for admin and migrations) even if the default Manager filters.

patterns.name: Test Case
patterns.intent: Classes inheriting from unittest.TestCase or Django's TestCase providing isolated test fixtures.
patterns.description: Python's unittest.TestCase provides test isolation via setUp/tearDown, assertion helpers (assertEqual, assertRaises, assertIn, etc.), and test discovery. Django's TestCase wraps each test in a database transaction that is rolled back after each test, providing fast database isolation. SimpleTestCase avoids any database access. TransactionTestCase commits to the database (slower but necessary for testing transaction behaviour).
patterns.when_to_apply: Use unittest.TestCase (or Django's subclass) for tests that need setUp/tearDown lifecycle, shared fixtures, or the full assertion library. For simpler unit tests without fixtures, plain functions (pytest-style) are often cleaner. Use Django's TestCase for views, models, and forms that touch the database. Use SimpleTestCase for views that do not touch the database (faster). Use pytest-django fixtures when you prefer the pytest composable fixture model over inheritance.

patterns.name: Plugin / Extension Point
patterns.intent: Allow third parties to extend core behaviour without modifying the core codebase.
patterns.description: Plugin / Extension Point is a structural pattern where the core system defines a stable interface (or abstract base class) that external or optional components can implement and register. The core discovers and invokes plugins at runtime without hard dependencies. Common mechanisms: Java ServiceLoader, Python entry_points (setuptools), Eclipse extension points, and simple naming convention-based discovery.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use when building a platform or framework that needs to be extensible by third parties or optional modules, when you want to decouple optional features from the core (keeping the core small), or when features need to be enabled/disabled per deployment without code changes. Requires careful API versioning — once published, the plugin interface is a commitment. Avoid over-engineering small applications with a plugin system — it adds significant complexity.

patterns.name: Evaluator Framework (Generated)
patterns.intent: Type-specialised expression evaluators generated from a template, each handling a specific combination of field storage types.
patterns.description: The Evaluator Framework is a performance optimisation pattern used in search engines (notably Elasticsearch/Lucene) where expression evaluation is specialised per storage type combination (DocValues+DocValues, DocValues+Source, etc.) to avoid virtual dispatch and boxing overhead. Classes are generated by a code generator rather than written by hand. Each generated class implements the same evaluator interface but has a type-specific fast path.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply in high-performance compute-intensive paths where virtual method dispatch or type-check overhead is measurable (confirmed by profiling). Use code generation to produce the variants rather than writing them by hand. Always ensure the full matrix of type combinations is generated — missing a variant causes ClassCastException at runtime (the canonical bug this framework guards against). Not appropriate for general business logic — the complexity is only justified in hot paths.

patterns.name: Serverless API Boundary
patterns.intent: Classes explicitly marked for public or internal serverless API exposure via @ServerlessScope.
patterns.description: The Serverless API Boundary pattern marks which classes and methods form the public contract of a serverless function or microservice. The @ServerlessScope annotation (or equivalent) signals to code review, tooling, and API gateways which surface area is externally reachable. This enables automated checks that public API classes do not accidentally expose internal types, change method signatures without versioning, or bypass authentication guards.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use in serverless or microservice architectures where it is important to explicitly delineate the public API surface from internal implementation. Pair with automated policy checks (e.g. via CodeKG ArchPolicy) that enforce rules on annotated classes. Particularly valuable in large teams where developers may not know which classes are externally reachable. Ensure all annotated classes are reviewed for security and backward compatibility before deployment.

patterns.name: God Class
patterns.intent: A class that knows too much or does too much — high method count, high field count, many dependencies.
patterns.description: A God Class (also called Blob or Large Class) violates the Single Responsibility Principle by accumulating too many responsibilities over time. It typically has a very high number of methods, fields, and dependencies. Every change to the system seems to require touching this class, creating a bottleneck. God classes are hard to understand, test, and modify safely, and they prevent parallel development because they are always the site of merge conflicts.
patterns.when_to_apply: This is an anti-pattern — it should be decomposed rather than applied. When detected, extract cohesive groups of methods and fields into separate classes with clear responsibilities. Common decomposition strategies: extract service classes, extract value objects, introduce domain-driven design aggregates. Apply the Single Responsibility Principle: a class should have only one reason to change. Detection thresholds (method_count_gt, field_count_gt) can be tuned per team.

patterns.name: Deprecated API
patterns.intent: Classes marked @Deprecated that are still actively used by other classes.
patterns.description: Deprecated APIs are classes, methods, or fields marked with @Deprecated (or @deprecated Javadoc) to signal that they will be removed in a future release. Continued use of deprecated APIs creates technical debt: when the deprecated item is eventually removed, all callers break. In large codebases, deprecated APIs sometimes persist for years because no one tracks the remaining usages. The @Deprecated annotation should be paired with a Javadoc comment explaining the replacement.
patterns.when_to_apply: This is an anti-pattern. When detected, prioritise migrating callers to the replacement API before the deprecated item is removed. Use @SuppressWarnings("deprecation") sparingly and only with a tracked issue to complete the migration. When deprecating your own APIs, provide a clear migration path in the Javadoc and set a concrete removal timeline. Use CodeKG policies to block new usages of specific deprecated classes from being added.

patterns.name: RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization)
patterns.intent: Bind resource lifetime to object lifetime so resources are always released, even under exceptions.
patterns.description: RAII ties the acquisition and release of a resource (memory, file handle, mutex, socket) to the constructor and destructor of a class. The C++ runtime guarantees destructors run when an object goes out of scope — including during stack unwinding from exceptions. This eliminates resource leaks without try/finally blocks. The standard library applies RAII throughout: std::unique_ptr, std::lock_guard, std::fstream, std::vector.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply to any class that owns a resource that must be released deterministically. Prefer RAII over manual new/delete, raw FILE*, or explicit lock()/unlock() calls. Use std::unique_ptr/shared_ptr instead of raw pointers. Use std::lock_guard or std::scoped_lock instead of manual mutex.lock()/unlock(). Write custom RAII wrappers for C APIs (e.g. wrapping a POSIX fd). Avoid RAII for resources that must outlive their lexical scope — use a shared_ptr or explicit lifetime management instead.

patterns.name: Pimpl (Pointer to Implementation)
patterns.intent: Hide private implementation details behind an opaque pointer to reduce compile-time coupling and binary compatibility issues.
patterns.description: Pimpl (also called d-pointer or Cheshire Cat) places all private data members and private methods in a separate implementation class whose definition lives only in the .cpp file. The public header only sees a forward-declared pointer. This breaks physical dependencies: changing private members no longer recompiles callers. It also enables ABI stability because the public class layout never changes. Widely used in Qt (QPrivate), KDE, and any library that needs to guarantee binary compatibility across releases.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use in library code that must maintain ABI compatibility across releases, when compilation times are unacceptably slow due to heavy private includes, or when you want to prevent clients from depending on private types. Pair with std::unique_ptr<Impl> for automatic cleanup. Avoid in performance-critical hot paths — the extra pointer indirection and heap allocation have measurable cost. Not needed for header-only libraries.

patterns.name: CRTP (Curiously Recurring Template Pattern)
patterns.intent: Achieve static (compile-time) polymorphism by having a base class template parameterised on its derived class.
patterns.description: CRTP uses the form `class Derived : public Base<Derived>`. The base class can call derived class methods via static_cast<Derived*>(this), achieving polymorphic dispatch without virtual functions. This eliminates vtable lookup overhead and enables zero-cost abstractions. Common uses: adding comparison operators via a mixin base (enable_shared_from_this), fluent builder interfaces, compile-time interface enforcement, and the Barton–Nackman trick for operator injection.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use in performance-sensitive code where virtual dispatch overhead is measurable, when the complete class hierarchy is known at compile time, and when adding reusable behaviour (operators, clone(), serialisation) to a family of types via a base class mixin. Avoid when the set of derived types is open (unknown at compile time) — that is the use case for runtime polymorphism. CRTP significantly increases template error message complexity; ensure the gain is worth the readability cost.

patterns.name: Rule of Five
patterns.intent: Classes that define any one of destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment, move constructor, or move assignment should explicitly define all five.
patterns.description: C++11 extended the Rule of Three (destructor + copy constructor + copy assignment) to the Rule of Five by adding move constructor and move assignment. If a class manages a resource (raw pointer, file handle, lock) and defines a custom destructor to release it, the compiler-generated copies will do a shallow copy of the pointer — causing double-free. Defining all five ensures correct resource ownership semantics. The Rule of Zero is the preferred modern alternative: own resources only through RAII types (unique_ptr, vector) so the compiler-generated five are always correct.
patterns.when_to_apply: Whenever a class owns a raw resource — raw pointer with new/delete, POSIX file descriptor, socket, mutex. Check: if you wrote a destructor, you need all five. Prefer the Rule of Zero: replace raw owning pointers with std::unique_ptr and the compiler generates correct semantics automatically. If a class should not be copyable (e.g. mutex wrapper), explicitly =delete the copy constructor and copy assignment. Always = default or = delete explicitly rather than relying on compiler generation rules.

patterns.name: C++ Singleton (Meyer's)
patterns.intent: Ensure a class has exactly one instance using a static local variable (Meyer's idiom) which is thread-safe since C++11.
patterns.description: The Meyer's Singleton uses a static local variable inside a static getInstance() method. In C++11 and later the initialization of static local variables is guaranteed to be thread-safe by the standard (magic statics). This is simpler and safer than double-checked locking. The singleton can be destroyed by the runtime at process exit, which can cause issues with global destructors — use Phoenix Singleton or std::atexit if destruction order matters.
patterns.when_to_apply: Use for truly global services with no useful alternative: logging sinks, configuration objects, hardware interface wrappers. In most cases, prefer dependency injection — passing the shared object through constructors — over global singletons. Singletons make unit testing hard because they carry state between tests. Avoid for anything that needs to be reset or replaced between tests. Consider std::call_once + std::once_flag for thread-safe one-time initialization that doesn't require a class.

patterns.name: Raw Owning Pointer
patterns.intent: Classes using raw pointers (T*) for ownership rather than std::unique_ptr or std::shared_ptr.
patterns.description: Raw owning pointers (T* where the class is responsible for deleting the object) are a primary source of memory leaks, use-after-free, and double-free bugs in C++. Modern C++ (C++11+) provides std::unique_ptr for exclusive ownership and std::shared_ptr for shared ownership, both of which release resources automatically and work correctly in the presence of exceptions. Raw pointers are still appropriate for non-owning references (observing pointers), but ownership must be expressed through smart pointers.
patterns.when_to_apply: This is an anti-pattern. Migrate raw owning pointers to std::unique_ptr (exclusive) or std::shared_ptr (shared). Use T* only for non-owning references. Apply static analysis tools (clang-tidy, cppcheck) and AddressSanitizer to catch remaining raw pointer bugs. In code reviews, require a comment explaining why a raw pointer is non-owning whenever T* appears as a class member. Add a CodeKG policy to flag classes with destructor + raw pointer fields.

patterns.name: Missing Virtual Destructor
patterns.intent: A class with virtual methods does not declare a virtual destructor, causing undefined behaviour when deleting via a base pointer.
patterns.description: Deleting a derived-class object through a base-class pointer is undefined behaviour if the base class does not have a virtual destructor. The derived destructor is never called, leaking any resources the derived class owns. This is a silent correctness bug: code compiles, runs, and often appears to work until a leak is detected by a sanitizer or the program is run under valgrind. The fix is trivial: declare `virtual ~Base() = default;` in any class that has virtual methods or is intended to be used polymorphically.
patterns.when_to_apply: This is an anti-pattern. Any class that has at least one virtual method (and is therefore intended to be used polymorphically) must declare a virtual destructor. If the class is not intended to be used as a base class, declare it `final` to prevent inheritance. If a base class destructor should not do anything, use `virtual ~Base() = default;`. Static analysis tools (clang-tidy: cppcoreguidelines-virtual-class-destructor) catch this automatically — add this check to CI.

patterns.name: Missing Evaluator Variant
patterns.intent: An evaluator family is missing a DocValuesAndDocValues variant, which causes ClassCastException at runtime when both fields use doc-values.
patterns.description: In the Evaluator Framework pattern, each expression evaluator must be generated for every combination of input field storage types (DocValues, Source, Constant). When a DocValuesAndDocValues variant is absent but DocValuesAndSource exists, the runtime falls back to an incorrect evaluator, causing a ClassCastException when both input fields happen to be stored as doc-values. This is a silent correctness bug — the code compiles and most tests pass, but fails on specific data configurations.
patterns.when_to_apply: This is an anti-pattern that should be prevented by the code generator. When detected: (1) add the missing variant to the code generator template, (2) regenerate all evaluators, (3) add a unit test that specifically exercises the DocValuesAndDocValues path. The code generator should enforce that all combinations are always produced. Add this as a CodeKG architectural policy to fail CI when a missing variant is detected.
